coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorescent biosensing platform based on CDs and AuNCs@CGO for patulin detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yao, Mingru
Zhan, Haosong
Liu, Lu
Gai, Teer
Zhao, Dongyue
Wei, Wu
coredata.dc:description:
Background Patulin (PAT) is a mycotoxin, usually found in fruit and their products, that can potentially be harmful to human health. In order to achieve rapid detection of mycotoxins and ensure the safety of food. This study reported a novel ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor for PAT detection. In this study, we used aptamer as the recognition element, Hybrid double stranded modified with fluorescent substances as the fluorescent donor, and AuNCs@CGO as the fluorescent acceptor. After the addition of PAT, the ratiometric fluorescence “turn on” response was exhibited.
Results The AuNCs@CGO are obtained by amide reaction between BSA-AuNCs and carboxylated graphene oxide (CGO). The prepared AuNCs@CGO can shorten the time of FRET effect and exhibit highly efficient quenching ability by adsorption effects (π-π stacking and electrostatic gravity) on the Aptamer-modified CDs. When the target PAT bound specifically to the CDs-apt, the fluorescence of the CDs-apt would recover, while the fluorescence of ROX modified cDNA remained unchanged. This ratiometric fluorescence response improved the accuracy of PAT detection. In addition, the proposed had good linearity for PAT in the range of 0.1–50 ng/mL with a limit of detection 0.16 ng/mL. The recovery of standard addition in grapes were 95.9%–105.4 %.
Significance An effective fluorescent detection method for PAT was constructed based on aptamer and nanomaterials. This new fluorescent biosensor has the characteristics of simple synthesis, easy operation, high sensitivity, strong selectivity and a low LOD, which may be a promising idea and platform for the detection of food safety hazard factors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Patulin
Detection
Ratiometric fluorescence aptasensor
FRET
Carboxylated graphene oxide

Bioconjugation of capture aptamer to CDs
Detection of PAT in real samples
Principle of ratiometric fluorescence aptasensor for the detection
Bioconjugation of CDs and aptamer using EDC/NHS
Performance of fluorescence quenching by AuNCs@CGO
Ratiometric fluorescence assay for patulin detection
Detection of PAT in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM image (a) and particle distribution (n = 40) (b) of CDs, the TEM image (c) and the particle size distribution (n = 40) of BSA-AuNCs.
(a) The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of AuNCs coupled on the surface of CGO. (b) FTIR spectra of GO, CGO, and AuNCs@CGO.

(a) UV–Vis absorbance spectra of CGO, AuNCs and AuNCs@CGO. (b) The UV–Vis absorbance spectrum of CDs, ssDNA, ssDNA modified CDs, and ssDNA-AuNCs@CGO. (c) Fluorescence intensity of CDs and ROX under different conditions.
(a) The fluorescence quenching properties of CDs and ROX with various amounts of AuNCs@CGO. (b) The fluorescence quenching efficiency with the incubation time.

(a) Fluorescence response of ratiometric signal probe in the presence of various concentrations of PAT (0.1–50 ng/mL). (b) Linear curve for PAT detection. (c) Specificity of ratiometric fluorescent biosensor toward PAT (20 ng/mL) and others (blank, ZEN, AFB1, OTA) at a concentration of 200 ng/mL.
Schematic illustration of a ratiometric fluorescence “turn-on” aptasensor based on FRET for patulin detection.
The sequences of Oligonucleotides applied in this study.
Comparison of the developed method with previously reported for the PAT detection.

row.entry: Dual-color enhanced AuNCs as fluorescence amplifiers
rGO and DNase I-assisted based FRET

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorescent biosensing platform based on CDs and AuNCs@CGO for patulin detection

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Food Science and Engineering, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao, 266109, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Patulin (PAT) is a mycotoxin, usually found in fruit and their products, that can potentially be harmful to human health. In order to achieve rapid detection of mycotoxins and ensure the safety of food. This study reported a novel ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor for PAT detection. In this study, we used aptamer as the recognition element, Hybrid double stranded modified with fluorescent substances as the fluorescent donor, and AuNCs@CGO as the fluorescent acceptor. After the addition of PAT, the ratiometric fluorescence “turn on” response was exhibited.

The AuNCs@CGO are obtained by amide reaction between BSA-AuNCs and carboxylated graphene oxide (CGO). The prepared AuNCs@CGO can shorten the time of FRET effect and exhibit highly efficient quenching ability by adsorption effects (π-π stacking and electrostatic gravity) on the Aptamer-modified CDs. When the target PAT bound specifically to the CDs-apt, the fluorescence of the CDs-apt would recover, while the fluorescence of ROX modified cDNA remained unchanged. This ratiometric fluorescence response improved the accuracy of PAT detection. In addition, the proposed had good linearity for PAT in the range of 0.1–50 ng/mL with a limit of detection 0.16 ng/mL. The recovery of standard addition in grapes were 95.9%–105.4 %.

An effective fluorescent detection method for PAT was constructed based on aptamer and nanomaterials. This new fluorescent biosensor has the characteristics of simple synthesis, easy operation, high sensitivity, strong selectivity and a low LOD, which may be a promising idea and platform for the detection of food safety hazard factors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Patulin (PAT) is a highly toxic mycotoxin metabolite usually produced from various fungal species, such as Penicillium expansum, Asperillus and Byssochlamys, mainly found from rotten fruits and their products [1], [2]. Mycotoxin contamination in food and agricultural products is a hot isuue worldwide and poses a high threat to human health and the economy [3]; [4]. Exposure to patulin can cause irreversible damage to the liver, intestines, and kidney organs in humans and animals and may cause teratogenicity, carcinogenicity, neurotoxicity and immunotoxicity to the human body [5–7]. Because of the high toxicity and its widespread presence of patulin in food, Europe, the United States and China CB 2761-2017 stipulate that the maximum residue limit of PAT in fruits was about 50 μg/kg [8,9].

At present, the traditional methods for the quantitative analysis of patulin mainly include high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and thin layer [10], HPLC coupled to conventional detection techniques [11], mass spectrometry [12], gas chromatography-mass spectrometry [13], etc. Although these methods are reproducible and sensitive, they rely on Large-scale Instruments, the cost is expensive, complex steps and they require professional operators. Besides, molecularly imprinted polymers [14], methods based on surface plasmon resonance [15], electrochemical and fluorescence sensors ([16]; Yan et al., 2022) are also reported. Lateral flow immunoassay (LFIA) is an effective assay which are based on antibody-antigen interaction. Although specificity of antibodies, their production process is complicated and expensive. Therefore, there is an urgant need to debelop methods with equivalent effects to reduce costs and increase sensitivity（[17]）.

In recent decades, DNA biosensors have attracted the attention of researchers for their ability to detect specific sequences quickly and accurately. Fluorescent biosensors have shown great potential and promise due to their rapid display and processing [18]. Fluorescent probes have become the main detection method in recent years due to their rapid response, high sensitivity, high selectivity, and simple operation (Jia er al., 2020 [19], Wang et al., 2021 [20]). Although fluorescent nanomaterials sensors for detection have been reported in many reports, most of them were single fluorescence signal, which is susceptible to interference from environmental impurities, making the results unreliable in practical applications [21]. Thus, compared to intensity-based ones, ratiometric fluorescent probes are more accurate, because they can eliminate errors by the two synergistic fluorescence emissions. Besides, ratiometric biosensors have less dependence on sensor concentrations, excitation intensity, and photobleaching (Youssef et al., 2019 [22]).

To construct fluorescent aptasensors, fluorescent dyes and synthetic chemically luminescent materials are typically modified at the end of the DNA oligonucleotide strand to provide signals as signaling molecules [18]. The fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) strategy is a technique that transfers the excited energy of the donor to the acceptor when the donor and acceptor are close enough (＜10 nm), resulting in the fluorescence intensity of the donor decreased. Besides, the energy transfer efficiency inversely correlates to the sixth power of the distance between the donor and acceptor, [23]. At present, the donors of FRET have evolved from organic dyes to fluorescent nanomaterials because of their excellent high photostability and tunable excitation by adjusting the size of nanostructures ([24]; [25]).

Graphene oxide (GO), is commonly applied in biosensing [26], antimicrobial [27] and bioimaging [28] fields. Selective chemical functionalization of GO surface is critical for the wider application of GO. The covalently linked (i.e., bioconjugation) of bioactive molecules through the oxygen-containing groups of GO is a major application in biological applications, where the carboxylic group is the most commonly used target, although the amount on the GO surface is small [29]. Therefore, it is necessary to carboxylate selectively the oxygenated functional groups on the GO surface to obtain carboxylated graphene oxide (CGO) that is more suitable for conjugation with biomolecules.

In this study, we developed a ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on FRET effect capable of detecting low concentrations of PAT. CDs are linked to aptamer to capture targets, and the target strand binds to the fluorescent dye-labeled complementary strand DNA as a signal probe for proportional fluorescence response and donor for FRET. The prepared AuNCs@CGO composites act as quenching agents, which can quench the fluorescence of FRET receptors in a short time. When the target is present, the high affinity of the aptamer to the target results in the unwinding of the double strands (dsDNA), the CDs-modified aptamer leaves the surface of AuNCs@CGO, and the fluorescence turning on with FRET effect weakened. The developed ratiometric biosensor is better for overcoming environmental factors and background signal interference, providing opportunities to construct sensitive, selective and effective FRET-based platforms for detection.

Patulin (PAT), ochratoxin A (OTA), Zearalenone (ZEN) and aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) were purchased from yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Bovine serum albumin (BSA) and EDC were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Graphene oxide (GO), Chloroauric acid trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O), NHS and Chloroacetic acid were supplied by McLean (Shanghai, China). Ethanol was obtained from Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd (China). 1 × PBS buffer (0.01 M, pH 7.2–7.4) was purchased from Solarbio Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Citric acid monohydrate, Urea, NaOH were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). GelRed stain, 50 × TAE buffer, DNA molecular weight standard Marker A (25-500bp) were all supplied by Sangon Biotech (Shanghai, China). All solutions were prepared using a Milli-Q water purification system (Sartorius, Germany). The PAT aptamer sequence and the complementary to the PAT strand (cDNA) were synthesized by Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and purified using HPLC. Their seq are shown in Table 1 .

Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a F-2700 Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Hitachi Ltd., Japan). The UV–vis absorbance measurements were performed on an Evolution 201 UV–Vis spectrophotometer 9Thermo Fisher, USA). The topography and the average size were verified by a JEM-2100 F transmission electron microscopy (TEM) instrument (Hitachi Ltd., Japan) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM). Gel electrophoresis experiments were performed by a Mini-Sub Cell T System (Bio-Rad Laboratories, Lnc. USA.). The gels of agarose gel electrophoresis (AGE) were imaged under GelDoc EZ (Bio-Rad, America) and processed by Image Lab software (Bio-Rad, America). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was used to investigate the bioconjugation and the results were recorded using a Nicolet iS 10 (Thermo Ltd., America).

CDs was synthesized as inspired by previous studies. Briefly, 0.5324 g of citric acid and 0.2496 g of urea were dissolved in a mixed solution of 20 mL of water and absolute ethanol（V/V = 3:1）and mixed evenly. Then transfer the mixed solution into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 160 °C for a period of 6hthe reaction liquid was cooled to room temperature. The obtained brown solution was collected by removing the large impurities through centrifugation at 12000 rpm. After that the resulting solution was dialyzed in a 1 kDa dialysis bag for 48 h to remove small molecules and the excess precursors. Finally, the CDs powder was obtained after freeze-drying under vacuum.

The obtained BSA-AuNCs was prepared according to the previous report [30]. A solution (10 mL) containing HauCl4·3H2O (5 mL, 10 mM) and BSA (5 mL, 50 mg/mL) was vigorously stirred at 37 °C. After 2 min, 5 mL NaOH solution (1M) was introduced, and the mixture was incubated overnight at 37 °C with vigorous shaking. Finally, the reaction solution changed color from light yellow to red, and to obtain purified BSA-AuNCs, the solution was dialyzed using Milli-Q water to remove the excess/unreacted impurities. The purified BSA-AuNCs were stored at 4 °C in a refrigerator.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carboxylated GO and AuNCs@CGO conjugates
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of Carboxylated graphene oxide (CGO) and AuNCs@CGO conjugates were described in detail in SI file. The carboxylated GO was prepared to refer to the method by Sun and coworkers [31]. Briefly, NaOH (1.2 g) and chloroacetic acid (1.0 g) were added sequentially to the GO suspension and stirred until completely dissolved to obtain CGO. The resulting CGO solution (2 mg/mL, 5 mL) was mixed with BSA-AuNCs (6 mL), followed by the addition of 40 mg EDC and 20 mg NHS for activation and shaking at 37 °C for 10 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bioconjugation of capture aptamer to CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The PAT probes were covalently attached to CDs using EDC/NHS as activating agents. Briefly, the dried CDs powder was resuspended in ultrapure water to get a 2 mg/mL concentration. EDC and NHS were added to 600 μL CDs (2 mg/mL) solution and then incubated for 1 h at 37 °C to achieve carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs. Then, 90 μL amino-modified PAT aptamer (10 μM) was added at 37 °C for 4 h in the dark. Finally, the sample of CDs-ssDNA probe was isolated and stored under 4 °C.

First, the fluorescence probe CDs modified PAT aptamer (150 μL) and 150 μL ROX modified cDNA (2 μM) were injected into a centrifuge tube, followed by the addition of 370 μL PBS buffer (0.01 M, PH 7.2–7.4). The obtained solution was incubated for 1 h at 37 °C to synthesize partially complementary double-stranded probes. Then, 100 μL PAT was added to react with the corresponding aptamer probes at 37 °C for 1h. After that, 130 μL AuNCs@CGO (2 mg/mL) solution was shaken and mixed at 25 °C for 20 min in the dark to quench the Fluorescence intensity and record fluorescence spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of PAT in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practical application of the ratiometric fluorescence aptasensor is evaluated by detecting patulin in grapes. First, different concentrations of patulin (1 ng/mL, 10 ng/mL, and 20 ng/mL) were added to 1 g broken grape samples (purchased from the local supermarket). Then 1 mL of the spiked sample was vortex mixed with 1 mL of ethy acetate. After centrifuging the mixture at 4000 rpm for 5 min, the supernatant was dried by nitrogen. The obtained dry powder sample was dissolved in PBS (1 mL, 0.01 M, pH 7.2–7.4) for later analysis and the photoluminescence spectra were recorded through recycling experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of ratiometric fluorescence aptasensor for the detection
ce:section.ce:para: The principle of the PAT detection aptasensor was illustrated in Scheme 1 . The quenching ability of AuNCs@CGO were employed to construct FRET strategy from signal probe (energy donor) to AuNCs@CGO (energy acceptor). The single-stranded portion of signal probe was adsorbed on the surface of AuNCs@CGO due to π-π accumulation and electrostatic attraction. Because of the close proximity of the acceptor and donor which leads to FRET mechanism, the fluorescence of hybridized DNA strands were quenched. Due to the higher affinity between the aptamer and the analyte, the addition of PAT weakened the adsorption between the ssDNA and the AuNCs@CGO, so that the PAT bound to the capture probe CDs-apt and detached from the AuNCs@CGO surface. The PAT concentration was detected by the fluorescence change of CDs, while the complementary chain fluorescence barely changed, so as to achieve the quantification of toxins.

The nanoparticle structure information of as-synthesized CDs and AuNCs were characterized by TEM measurement. As shown in Fig. 1 (a), CDs were evenly dispersed and presented spherical shaped. The particle distribution analyses (Fig. 1(b)) of CDs demonstrate a mean diameter of 4 nm. As presented in Fig. 1(c) and (d), morphology and particle distribution investigations of BSA-AuNCs were well dispersed, spherical shaped and with a mean particle size of 2.2 nm. In addition, the surface groups of CDs, BSA-AuNCs were characterized by FT-IR measurement (Fig. S1(a)), and the telescopic vibration O–H/N–H is observed around 3424-3295 cm−1. The absorption peak around 1541 cm−1 also contributed to the vibration of N–H (amide II band) [32]. Thus, the results proved that BSA-AuNCs contain many amino groups. As for CDs, the peak around 1720-1595 cm−1 is attributed to the –CO vibration of the carboxyl group which can coupled to amino aptamers. The prepared CDs were performed as fluorescence donors of FRET effect and the fluorescence emission characteristics of CDs were recorded. As shown in Fig. S2 the common fluorescence emission peak at 450 nm at different excitation wavelengths of the CDs, and the highest emission peak at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm.

As shown in Fig. 2 (a) , the images of TEM demonstrated that AuNCs with a diameter of 2.2 nm were uniformly attached to the surface of CGO sheets. To further confirm the synthesis of AuNCs@CGO, FTIR has been used in measuring the carboxylation of graphene oxide and the successful bioconjugation of AuNCs onto CGO. The spectra of GO, CGO and AuNCs@CGO are depicted in Fig. 2 (b). The strong peak in the range of 3200 cm−1 to 3500 cm−1 corresponds to O–H stretching vibration of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups [33]. The peaks at 1630 cm−1, 1101 cm−1 of GO are attributed to –CO stretching and C–O bending, respectively [34]. Compared with GO, the carboxyl characteristic absorption peak of CGO at 3431 cm−1 was significantly stronger than that of GO, which proved the successful carboxylation of GO. In addition, the peaks at 1630 cm−1 to 1690 cm−1 and around 1500 cm-1 corresponded to –CO stretching (amide bond) and N–H vibration of I and II, verifying the formation of –CO–NH- (peptide bond) between CGO and AuNCs [35].

The absorbance of samples of CGO, AuNCs and AuNCs@CGO, have been measured by UV–vis spectrometer (Fig. 3 (a) ). The CGO has a typical absorbance peak at 230 nm, corresponding to the π-π∗ transition of aromatic π electrons. The small peak at 300 nm is associated with the n-π∗ transition of the CO bond of the carboxyl group on CGO [36]. AuNCs have a typical narrow absorption shoulder at 280 nm, so the changes in the absorption shoulder of AuNCs@CGO proved that the AuNCs decorated onto CGO successfully. The reason for a shift in the position of 260 nm after binding may be due to the proximity of the characteristic peaks of the CGO and AuNCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bioconjugation of CDs and aptamer using EDC/NHS
ce:section.ce:para: The coupling of CDs and aptamer was verified by 3 % agarose electrophoresis, the product stained with GelRed was analyzed as shown in Fig. S3. Lanes 1, 2, and 3 were DNA marker, PAT aptamer, and PAT aptamer incubation with CDs, respectively. The gel imaging showed that the electrophoretic band of Lane 3 was shallower than Lane 2, indicating that the concentration of PAT aptamer was decreased, proving that PAT aptamer was successfully connected with CDs. In addition, as shown in Fig. 3 (b), CDs show two significant absorption peaks in UV–vis spectrum centering at 237 nm and 336 nm, which is caused by the multifunctional groups and the nitrogen doping into CDs [37]. The blue curve shows three absorption peaks, one of them at 260 nm presents the absorbance of ssDNA without obvious shift, and the other two peaks are attributed to CDs. The results indicate the ssDNA has been successfully bioconjugated to CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of fluorescence quenching by AuNCs@CGO
ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials such as CDs and BSA-AuNCs were used in FRET sensing system to improve sensing performance. In this study, CDs/ROX as the donor and AuNCs@CGO as the fluorescence acceptors. GO possesses large surface area, fluorescence quenching ability, surface functionalizability and other superior properties leading to its wide application. To explore the feasibility of the experiment and prove AuNCs@CGO can be used as an effective quencher for fluorescent materials. As displayed in Fig. 3 (c), the fluorescent signal of CDs/ROX were decreased significantly with the addition of AuNCs@CGO. The strong fluorescence intensity of capture probes were restored in the presence of patulin because of the elimination of the weak bond between CDs-apt and AuNCs@CGO that obstructs the FRET effect. In the process of FRET, the energy transfer between fluorescence materials and quencher is based on the overlap between the fluorescent donor emission band and the fluorescent acceptot absoptiong band [38].

The mechanism of FRET quenching also relies on the distance of very close proximity (＜10 nm）between the donor and the quencher. When CDs-apt acts as the donor, the single-strand DNA (ssDNA) is rapidly absorbed and deposited on the GO through π-π∗ interaction, while the dsDNA formed cannot be adsorbed with GO ([39]; [40]). As shown in Fig. 3 (b), the absorption peak at 260 nm verifies the successful attachment of the ssDNA part to the surface of the AuNCs@CGO. Furthermore, decorating AuNCs onto the CGO reveals the special capabilities of new hybrid material, which can be applied to the field of sensors. Compared with GO, AuNCs@CGO nanocomposite materials can significantly quench the fluorescence of CDs in a short time, thus exhibiting high optical activity for fluorescence quenching of CDs that can be utilized as a good candidate for optical sensors.

The optimization of experimental conditions was explored at the optimal excitation wavelengths of CDs and ROX. The photoluminescence spectra of ROX indicate that 550 nm was chosen to excite the strongest emission intensity of the fluorochrome ROX at 604 nm (Fig. S4). The fluorescence turn-on analytical performance is based on the FRET interaction between fluorescent probe and AuNCs@CGO. In this situation, optimization studies are crucial to obtain a high performance. The AuNCs@CGO, as quencher, has a significant correlation with the fluorescence response of the experiment. Therefore, the effect of different amounts of AuNCs@CGO (0, 10, 40, 70, 100, 130, 160, 200 μL) on the degree of fluorescence quenching of fluorescent probe was explored. Fig. 4 (a) displays the changes in fluorescence intensity of CDs and ROX with the introduction of AuNCs@CGO, and the Fluorescence intensity gradually decreased with the increase of AuNCs@CGO. When the amounts of AuNCs reach 130 μL, the fluorescence quenching remained basically stable or presented a pretty slight decrease. In addition, too much AuNCs@CGO may cause non-specific adsorption of the subsequent target analyte, which is detrimental to the specific binding of the aptamer to the target. Thereby, 130 μL of AuNCs@CGO was mixed with DNA hybridization double-stranded solution in the following experiments.

The optimized concentration of AuNCs@CGO was incubated with PAT fluorescent probe to explore the fluorescence quenching efficiency in a different time. As displayed in Fig. 4(b), AuNCs@CGO greatly shortened the fluorescence quenching time. The fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0–F/F0) both remained stable after 20 min, indicating the sensor probe and AuNCs@CGO complexes were fully reactive. Therefore, 20 min was chosen as the optimized quenching time for later experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence assay for patulin detection
ce:section.ce:para: The ratiometric fluorescence turn-on response for patulin based on the FRET efficiency was used to explore the sensitivity and general applicability of the detection method. To quantitate patulin, the developed ratiometric fluorescent probe based on the AuNCs@CGO complexes were applied under the above optimal quenching time and AuNCs addition amount to record the changes in ratiometric fluorescence intensity. The photoluminescence spectra of the fluorescence probe changes with different concentrations of patulin were recorded in Fig. 5 (a) . Under the excitation of 360 nm and 550 nm, two obvious peaks can be recorded, reflected in the fluorescence peak of CDs around 450 nm and the peak of ROX near 604 nm, respectively. It was clearly seen that the fluorescence intensity at the wavelength of 450 nm gradually enhanced after the injection of patulin. However, the peaks intensity of ROX at 604 nm were almost unchanged. These results showed that the FRET interaction between the capture probe strand and AuNCs@CGO was weakened after the addition of the target, and fluorescence of the ROX-modified cDNA short chain may have been quenched completely due to its proximity to the surface of AuNCs@CGO. In this situation, the fluorescence of ROX is almost unchanged after the double-stranded break.

As shown in Fig. 5 (b), the ratiometric response (I450/I605) increased with the increasing amounts of patulin. Plotting the ratiometric fluorescence intensity with the concentration of patulin obtained a good linear relationship, with a correlation function of Y = 0.3683lg(X)+1.952 (R2 = 0.99171) and the linear dynamic response range can be yielded from 0.1 to 50 ng/mL. According to European countries and China regulations, the maximum residue limit for PAT in fruit was 50 μg/kg (Li et al., 2018 [41]; Cimen et al., 2021 [9]). As shown in Table 2 , we summarized some of the existing patulin detection methods, and it can be seen that the developed ratiometric fluorescent probe detection study has good analytical performance and a wide response range for patulin detection. According to the formula of 3σ/S (σ is standard deviation of blanks, S is the slope of the standard curve). As compared in Table 2, the limit of our detection was 0.16 ng/mL, which is comparable to the above methods or even lower.

In order to elucidate the selectivity of this synthesized ratiometric fluorescent turn-on probe, other possible interfering mycotoxins under the same conditions and procedures were considered, including ZEN, AFB1, OTA, and Blank. The concentration of other mycotoxins was 200 ng/mL, while the concentration of PAT was set to 20 ng/mL. As shown in Fig. 5 (c), the mentioned above toxins have slight interference with the detection, only PAT produces a significant ratiometric fluorescence response, while the concentration of other mycotoxins was 10 folds than that of PAT. This can be attributed to the fact that the high affinity between the aptamer and the target facilitates the capture of the target by the aptamer strand. The adsorption of single-stranded aptamer on the AuNCs@CGO was reduced with the distance between them increasing and the FRET effect weakened. Therefore, the synthesized ratiometric fluorescent probe has good selectivity for PAT detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of PAT in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: We conducted a real sample analusis using fluorescent aptasensor based on FRET between ratiometric fluorescent probes and AuNCs@CGO. We analzed grape samples with three different concentrations of PAT. As shown in Table S1, when the corresponding spiked concentration in the sample was 1 ng/mL, 10 ng/mL, 20 ng/mL, the recovery rate of patulin in grape was 95.9 %–105.4 %, and RSD varying from 2.43 % to 5.86 %.

In summary, the adsorption between the fluorescent probe ssDNA and the AuNCs@CGO is due to the accumulation of π-π stacking and electrostatic attraction to form complexes, resulting in fluorescence quenching of the fluorescent probe because of the FRET effect. In this study, a fluorescent aptasensor in detecting PAT with a nanomaterial-based was developed. Ratiometric fluorescence intensity was increased by the addition of PAT. Specific binding of the target to the capture probe in the ratiometric fluorescent probe reduces the FRET effect, resulting in the recovery of CDs fluorescence. Under optimal conditions, this study could be successfully used to detect PAT from 0.1 to 50 ng/mL with a detection limit as low as 0.16 ng/mL. Moreover, the aptasensor exhibited excellent storage stability and specificity with quick response. It is expected that the proposed approach and the developed composite material may greatly promote its application in the development of novel detection methods and various fields.

This work was financially supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China [No. 2022YFF1102200], National Natural Science Foundation of China [No. U22A20541], the High-level Talent Start-up Fund from Qingdao Agricultural University, Science & Technology Specific Projects in Agricultural High-tech Industrial Demonstration Area of the Yellow River Delta (2022SZX26).

Mingru Yao: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Haosong Zhan: Investigation. Lu Liu: Investigation. Teer Gai: Investigation. Dongyue Zhao: Investigation. Wu Wei: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
